title: 1 Tir — St. Adam & The Assembly
narrative:
narrative": "narrative\": \"On the first day of Tir, the Church again sets the figure of the first man, Adam, at the threshold of a new month, as she set him at the threshold of Tahsas. Yet the light in which Adam is regarded on this first day of Tir is a different light: where Tahsas opened in the shadow of his exile and the longing of his descendants for a Redeemer, Tir opens with Adam viewed from the far side of the Nativity, in the radiance that is now streaming outward from the cave of Bethlehem toward the Jordan River. Adam's name — drawn from the dust of the adamah, the red earth — is the name that the Second Adam has now taken upon Himself, and the Church meditates on this first day upon the dignity restored to the clay of Adam's making by the Word who took it as His own. The month of Tir is the month of the public manifestation; and Adam, the father of the race that is about to be publicly claimed by God at the waters of baptism, stands as the silent patriarch presiding over all that follows.

The holy Protomartyr Stephen, whose monthly commemoration returns on this first day of Tir, carries into this new month the ardor of his witness at the boundary between the old dispensation and the new. Stephen, whose name in the Greek means 'crown,' was the first of the baptized to seal his faith with blood, and his martyrdom has always been understood by the fathers as itself a kind of epiphany: the heavens opened above him as they would open above the Jordan, and he saw the glory of God and Jesus standing at the right hand of the Father. The vision granted to Stephen at his martyrdom is the same vision that the feast of Timqat will celebrate in its cosmic dimension eleven days hence — the revelation of the Trinity in the theater of the visible world.

Saint Basil the Great, the Cappadocian doctor and Archbishop of Caesarea, whose feast the universal Church celebrates on the first day of January and whose memory the Ethiopian Synaxarium honors on this opening day of Tir, was among the most comprehensive theological intellects of the patristic era. His name Basil — from the Greek basileus, meaning 'king' — befitted one who ruled the theological discourse of his century with sovereign authority, defending the full divinity of the Holy Spirit against the Pneumatomachians and articulating the Trinitarian theology that makes the feast of Timqat comprehensible. It was Basil who taught that the Baptism of the Lord at the Jordan was the paradigmatic revelation of the Holy Trinity: the Son standing in the water, the Spirit descending as a dove, the Father speaking from the opened heaven — and this Trinity, Basil insisted, is one in substance, three in person, undivided in glory.

The theological seal of this first day of Tir is the mystery of the public claim. Tahsas was a month of interior mystery — a Child hidden in a cave, known to shepherds and wise men and a handful of the poor. Tir is the month in which that hiddenness gives way to manifestation: the same Lord who lay in the manger will stand in the Jordan and be proclaimed before heaven and earth as the beloved Son. Adam, Stephen, and Basil together frame this transition: Adam is the fallen creature about to be publicly reclaimed; Stephen is the witness who already sees the glory that Timqat will proclaim; Basil is the theologian who ensures that what is seen at the Jordan is understood in its full Trinitarian depth. The month opens, and the Light moves toward the water.
